_meta.description: 50 RN lessons + 200 RN exam questions generated from rn-topic-manifest.json (static, no API)

lessons.title: Myocardial Infarction (STEMI / NSTEMI)
lessons.summary: Exam-focused review of Myocardial Infarction (STEMI / NSTEMI) for RN candidates in the United States context, with emphasis on assessment cues, priority interventions, monitoring, and patient safety. High-yield cues include ischemic chest discomfort patterns, serial troponins and ECG evolution, time-to-reperfusion language, and bleeding risk when antithrombotics are used. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.
learningObjectives: Describe priority assessment findings and monitoring parameters for Myocardial Infarction (STEMI / NSTEMI) on a medical-surgical or step-down unit.
Explain evidence-informed nursing interventions, including when to escalate care and how to communicate using SBAR.
Identify common exam distractors related to Myocardial Infarction (STEMI / NSTEMI) and distinguish safe nursing actions from unsafe choices.
Apply clinical judgment to integrated scenarios involving Cardiovascular priorities.

sections.heading: Clinical overview
sections.body: Myocardial Infarction (STEMI / NSTEMI) is a high-yield topic for RN licensing exams because it blends pathophysiology, assessment, interventions, and safety. Nurses are tested on recognizing instability early, selecting the best first action, and anticipating complications rather than memorizing rare trivia. Keep the cardiovascular lens in mind: trends beat single numbers, and airway–breathing–circulation threats outrank comfort tasks when priorities compete. Topic focus: High-yield cues include ischemic chest discomfort patterns, serial troponins and ECG evolution, time-to-reperfusion language, and bleeding risk when antithrombotics are used. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology and risk factors (exam lens)
sections.body: Focus on mechanisms that create predictable nursing priorities: tissue oxygen delivery, fluid shifts, inflammation, end-organ perfusion, and medication-related vulnerabilities. Exam items often pair a subtle trend (rising heart rate, narrowing pulse pressure, new confusion, or progressive work of breathing) with a distractor that sounds reasonable but delays escalation. Ask yourself what would harm the patient fastest if ignored, and what assessment data would change your next action within minutes. Reinforce: High-yield cues include ischemic chest discomfort patterns, serial troponins and ECG evolution, time-to-reperfusion language, and bleeding risk when antithrombotics are used.

sections.heading: Assessment and monitoring
sections.body: Build a mental checklist for bedside assessment: level of consciousness and airway protection, work of breathing and oxygenation, perfusion and urine output when relevant, pain and sedation interplay, infection cues, and high-alert medication effects. Tie monitoring to the plan of care: what parameter, how often, what threshold triggers notification, and what you would say in SBAR. For United States learners, be fluent in both common unit conventions used in stems and the underlying physiology so unit swaps do not break your reasoning.

sections.heading: Interventions, collaboration, and safety
sections.body: Prioritize interventions that stabilize the patient, prevent injury, and preserve dignity: oxygen and airway support as indicated, vascular access and labs as ordered, medication administration with rights verification, fall and bleeding precautions, infection prevention, and patient education that matches readiness to learn. Delegation items reward knowing what can be assigned to UAP/PSW versus what requires RN judgment, including follow-up supervision. Documentation should reflect assessments, notifications, and patient responses—not tasks performed without verification.

sections.heading: Exam strategy and distractor patterns
sections.body: Expect prioritization stems with multiple “urgent-sounding” patients: choose the option that addresses airway, breathing, circulation, or rapid neurologic decline first. For medication items, watch for reversed priorities (treating a number without symptoms), duplicate therapy, ignored allergies, and monitoring omissions for high-risk classes. SATA items reward comprehensive safe nursing: select every true nursing action, not only the “most important” one. When uncertain, eliminate options that delay assessment, withhold needed escalation, or violate scope and standards of practice.

examTips: If two answers look correct, pick the action that assesses first when assessment data is missing—unless the stem already confirms a life threat that requires immediate intervention.
For medication safety, link high-alert drugs to monitoring (labs, vitals, sedation, bleeding) rather than memorizing isolated facts.
SATA: treat each option as true/false; do not stop after finding two correct answers if more are safe and indicated.
Region tip (US): read units carefully and translate glucose or lab values mentally when the stem uses mg/dL conventions.

lessons.title: Ischemic Stroke and tPA Administration
lessons.summary: Exam-focused review of Ischemic Stroke and tPA Administration for RN candidates in the United States context, with emphasis on assessment cues, priority interventions, monitoring, and patient safety. Focus on last-known-well timing, NIHSS-style severity language without inventing a score, hemorrhagic conversion precautions, and blood pressure goals as commonly tested in acute ischemic stroke care. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

Explain evidence-informed nursing interventions, including when to escalate care and how to communicate using SBAR.
Identify common exam distractors related to Ischemic Stroke and tPA Administration and distinguish safe nursing actions from unsafe choices.
Apply clinical judgment to integrated scenarios involving Neurological priorities.

lessons.title: Hypertensive Crisis and Emergency
lessons.summary: Exam-focused review of Hypertensive Crisis and Emergency for RN candidates in the Canada context, with emphasis on assessment cues, priority interventions, monitoring, and patient safety. Separate hypertensive emergency (end-organ symptoms) from urgency; exams test first-line assessment for neurologic, cardiac, and renal target-organ damage and safe escalation. Use Canadian context where relevant: SI labs (mmol/L for glucose when applicable), kPa for blood gases when used, CTAS triage language for emergency scenarios, RPN/LPN scope paired with RN accountability, and provincial interprofessional standards. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.
learningObjectives: Describe priority assessment findings and monitoring parameters for Hypertensive Crisis and Emergency on a medical-surgical or step-down unit.
Explain evidence-informed nursing interventions, including when to escalate care and how to communicate using SBAR.
Identify common exam distractors related to Hypertensive Crisis and Emergency and distinguish safe nursing actions from unsafe choices.
Apply clinical judgment to integrated scenarios involving Cardiovascular priorities.

lessons.title: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS)
lessons.summary: Exam-focused review of Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS) for RN candidates in the United States context, with emphasis on assessment cues, priority interventions, monitoring, and patient safety. ARDS is tested through refractory hypoxemia concepts, lung-protective ventilation principles, prone positioning as a concept, and preventing ventilator-associated complications. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

lessons.title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)
lessons.summary: Exam-focused review of Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) for RN candidates in the Canada context, with emphasis on assessment cues, priority interventions, monitoring, and patient safety. DKA stems emphasize ketosis, anion gap, potassium shifts during insulin therapy, fluid resuscitation concepts, and frequent reassessment rather than cookbook numbers without orders. Use Canadian context where relevant: SI labs (mmol/L for glucose when applicable), kPa for blood gases when used, CTAS triage language for emergency scenarios, RPN/LPN scope paired with RN accountability, and provincial interprofessional standards. lessons.title: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS)
lessons.summary: Exam-focused review of Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS) for RN candidates in the United States context, with emphasis on assessment cues, priority interventions, monitoring, and patient safety. HHS is tested via profound dehydration, hyperosmolarity, neurologic changes, slower onset than DKA, and careful correction concepts with monitoring priorities. Exam items often pair a subtle trend (rising heart rate, narrowing pulse pressure, new confusion, or progressive work of breathing) with a distractor that sounds reasonable but delays escalation. Ask yourself what would harm the patient fastest if ignored, and what assessment data would change your next action within minutes. Reinforce: COPD exacerbation stems include increased work of breathing, infection triggers, controlled oxygen in chronic CO2 retainers as a concept, and bronchodilator therapy safety.

sections.heading: Assessment and monitoring
sections.body: Build a mental checklist for bedside assessment: level of consciousness and airway protection, work of breathing and oxygenation, perfusion and urine output when relevant, pain and sedation interplay, infection cues, and high-alert medication effects. Tie monitoring to the plan of care: what parameter, how often, what threshold triggers notification, and what you would say in SBAR. For Canada learners, be fluent in both common unit conventions used in stems and the underlying physiology so unit swaps do not break your reasoning.

sections.heading: Interventions, collaboration, and safety
sections.body: Prioritize interventions that stabilize the patient, prevent injury, and preserve dignity: oxygen and airway support as indicated, vascular access and labs as ordered, medication administration with rights verification, fall and bleeding precautions, infection prevention, and patient education that matches readiness to learn. Delegation items reward knowing what can be assigned to UAP/PSW versus what requires RN judgment, including follow-up supervision. Documentation should reflect assessments, notifications, and patient responses—not tasks performed without verification.

sections.heading: Exam strategy and distractor patterns
sections.body: Expect prioritization stems with multiple “urgent-sounding” patients: choose the option that addresses airway, breathing, circulation, or rapid neurologic decline first. For medication items, watch for reversed priorities (treating a number without symptoms), duplicate therapy, ignored allergies, and monitoring omissions for high-risk classes. SATA items reward comprehensive safe nursing: select every true nursing action, not only the “most important” one. When uncertain, eliminate options that delay assessment, withhold needed escalation, or violate scope and standards of practice.

examTips: If two answers look correct, pick the action that assesses first when assessment data is missing—unless the stem already confirms a life threat that requires immediate intervention.
For medication safety, link high-alert drugs to monitoring (labs, vitals, sedation, bleeding) rather than memorizing isolated facts.
SATA: treat each option as true/false; do not stop after finding two correct answers if more are safe and indicated.
Region tip (CA): read units carefully and translate glucose or lab values mentally when the stem uses mmol/L conventions.

sections.heading: Clinical overview
sections.body: Wound Care and Pressure Injury Prevention is a high-yield topic for RN licensing exams because it blends pathophysiology, assessment, interventions, and safety. Nurses are tested on recognizing instability early, selecting the best first action, and anticipating complications rather than memorizing rare trivia. Keep the integumentary lens in mind: trends beat single numbers, and airway–breathing–circulation threats outrank comfort tasks when priorities compete. Topic focus: Pressure injury staging concepts, moisture/incontinence management, turning schedules, nutrition as a contributor, and wound assessment documentation appear frequently. Use Canadian context where relevant: SI labs (mmol/L for glucose when applicable), kPa for blood gases when used, CTAS triage language for emergency scenarios, RPN/LPN scope paired with RN accountability, and provincial interprofessional standards. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology and risk factors (exam lens)
sections.body: Focus on mechanisms that create predictable nursing priorities: tissue oxygen delivery, fluid shifts, inflammation, end-organ perfusion, and medication-related vulnerabilities. Exam items often pair a subtle trend (rising heart rate, narrowing pulse pressure, new confusion, or progressive work of breathing) with a distractor that sounds reasonable but delays escalation. Ask yourself what would harm the patient fastest if ignored, and what assessment data would change your next action within minutes. Reinforce: Pressure injury staging concepts, moisture/incontinence management, turning schedules, nutrition as a contributor, and wound assessment documentation appear frequently.

sections.heading: Assessment and monitoring
sections.body: Build a mental checklist for bedside assessment: level of consciousness and airway protection, work of breathing and oxygenation, perfusion and urine output when relevant, pain and sedation interplay, infection cues, and high-alert medication effects. Tie monitoring to the plan of care: what parameter, how often, what threshold triggers notification, and what you would say in SBAR. For Canada learners, be fluent in both common unit conventions used in stems and the underlying physiology so unit swaps do not break your reasoning.

sections.heading: Interventions, collaboration, and safety
sections.body: Prioritize interventions that stabilize the patient, prevent injury, and preserve dignity: oxygen and airway support as indicated, vascular access and labs as ordered, medication administration with rights verification, fall and bleeding precautions, infection prevention, and patient education that matches readiness to learn. Delegation items reward knowing what can be assigned to UAP/PSW versus what requires RN judgment, including follow-up supervision. Documentation should reflect assessments, notifications, and patient responses—not tasks performed without verification.

sections.heading: Exam strategy and distractor patterns
sections.body: Expect prioritization stems with multiple “urgent-sounding” patients: choose the option that addresses airway, breathing, circulation, or rapid neurologic decline first. For medication items, watch for reversed priorities (treating a number without symptoms), duplicate therapy, ignored allergies, and monitoring omissions for high-risk classes. SATA items reward comprehensive safe nursing: select every true nursing action, not only the “most important” one. When uncertain, eliminate options that delay assessment, withhold needed escalation, or violate scope and standards of practice.

examTips: If two answers look correct, pick the action that assesses first when assessment data is missing—unless the stem already confirms a life threat that requires immediate intervention.
For medication safety, link high-alert drugs to monitoring (labs, vitals, sedation, bleeding) rather than memorizing isolated facts.
SATA: treat each option as true/false; do not stop after finding two correct answers if more are safe and indicated.
Region tip (CA): read units carefully and translate glucose or lab values mentally when the stem uses mmol/L conventions.

lessons.title: Urinary Tract Infection and Catheter-Associated UTI
lessons.summary: Exam-focused review of Urinary Tract Infection and Catheter-Associated UTI for RN candidates in the United States context, with emphasis on assessment cues, priority interventions, monitoring, and patient safety. UTI and CAUTI items reward catheter necessity review, specimen collection teaching, antibiotic stewardship concepts, and sepsis vigilance in older adults. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

Explain evidence-informed nursing interventions, including when to escalate care and how to communicate using SBAR.
Identify common exam distractors related to Urinary Tract Infection and Catheter-Associated UTI and distinguish safe nursing actions from unsafe choices.
Apply clinical judgment to integrated scenarios involving Renal priorities.

sections.heading: Clinical overview
sections.body: Diuretic Therapy: Loop, Thiazide, and Potassium-Sparing is a high-yield topic for RN licensing exams because it blends pathophysiology, assessment, interventions, and safety. Nurses are tested on recognizing instability early, selecting the best first action, and anticipating complications rather than memorizing rare trivia. Keep the renal lens in mind: trends beat single numbers, and airway–breathing–circulation threats outrank comfort tasks when priorities compete. Topic focus: Diuretics are tested through electrolyte loss (especially potassium), orthostasis, renal function trends, and ototoxicity risk with rapid IV loops as a concept. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology and risk factors (exam lens)
sections.body: Focus on mechanisms that create predictable nursing priorities: tissue oxygen delivery, fluid shifts, inflammation, end-organ perfusion, and medication-related vulnerabilities. Exam items often pair a subtle trend (rising heart rate, narrowing pulse pressure, new confusion, or progressive work of breathing) with a distractor that sounds reasonable but delays escalation. Ask yourself what would harm the patient fastest if ignored, and what assessment data would change your next action within minutes. Reinforce: Diuretics are tested through electrolyte loss (especially potassium), orthostasis, renal function trends, and ototoxicity risk with rapid IV loops as a concept.

sections.heading: Assessment and monitoring
sections.body: Build a mental checklist for bedside assessment: level of consciousness and airway protection, work of breathing and oxygenation, perfusion and urine output when relevant, pain and sedation interplay, infection cues, and high-alert medication effects. For United States learners, be fluent in both common unit conventions used in stems and the underlying physiology so unit swaps do not break your reasoning.

sections.heading: Interventions, collaboration, and safety
sections.body: Prioritize interventions that stabilize the patient, prevent injury, and preserve dignity: oxygen and airway support as indicated, vascular access and labs as ordered, medication administration with rights verification, fall and bleeding precautions, infection prevention, and patient education that matches readiness to learn. Delegation items reward knowing what can be assigned to UAP/PSW versus what requires RN judgment, including follow-up supervision. Documentation should reflect assessments, notifications, and patient responses—not tasks performed without verification.

sections.heading: Exam strategy and distractor patterns
sections.body: Expect prioritization stems with multiple “urgent-sounding” patients: choose the option that addresses airway, breathing, circulation, or rapid neurologic decline first. For medication items, watch for reversed priorities (treating a number without symptoms), duplicate therapy, ignored allergies, and monitoring omissions for high-risk classes. SATA items reward comprehensive safe nursing: select every true nursing action, not only the “most important” one. When uncertain, eliminate options that delay assessment, withhold needed escalation, or violate scope and standards of practice.

examTips: If two answers look correct, pick the action that assesses first when assessment data is missing—unless the stem already confirms a life threat that requires immediate intervention.
For medication safety, link high-alert drugs to monitoring (labs, vitals, sedation, bleeding) rather than memorizing isolated facts.
SATA: treat each option as true/false; do not stop after finding two correct answers if more are safe and indicated.
Region tip (US): read units carefully and translate glucose or lab values mentally when the stem uses mg/dL conventions.

sections.heading: Interventions, collaboration, and safety

sections.heading: Exam strategy and distractor patterns
sections.body: Expect prioritization stems with multiple “urgent-sounding” patients: choose the option that addresses airway, breathing, circulation, or rapid neurologic decline first. sections.heading: Pathophysiology and risk factors (exam lens)
sections.body: Focus on mechanisms that create predictable nursing priorities: tissue oxygen delivery, fluid shifts, inflammation, end-organ perfusion, and medication-related vulnerabilities. Exam items often pair a subtle trend (rising heart rate, narrowing pulse pressure, new confusion, or progressive work of breathing) with a distractor that sounds reasonable but delays escalation. Ask yourself what would harm the patient fastest if ignored, and what assessment data would change your next action within minutes. Reinforce: Shock types are tested through cold/warm clues as concepts, lactate, fluid responsiveness as a principle, and identifying cardiogenic versus distributive patterns at a high level.

sections.heading: Assessment and monitoring
sections.body: Build a mental checklist for bedside assessment: level of consciousness and airway protection, work of breathing and oxygenation, perfusion and urine output when relevant, pain and sedation interplay, infection cues, and high-alert medication effects. For United States learners, be fluent in both common unit conventions used in stems and the underlying physiology so unit swaps do not break your reasoning.

sections.heading: Interventions, collaboration, and safety
sections.body: Prioritize interventions that stabilize the patient, prevent injury, and preserve dignity: oxygen and airway support as indicated, vascular access and labs as ordered, medication administration with rights verification, fall and bleeding precautions, infection prevention, and patient education that matches readiness to learn. Delegation items reward knowing what can be assigned to UAP/PSW versus what requires RN judgment, including follow-up supervision. Documentation should reflect assessments, notifications, and patient responses—not tasks performed without verification.

sections.heading: Exam strategy and distractor patterns
sections.body: Expect prioritization stems with multiple “urgent-sounding” patients: choose the option that addresses airway, breathing, circulation, or rapid neurologic decline first. For medication items, watch for reversed priorities (treating a number without symptoms), duplicate therapy, ignored allergies, and monitoring omissions for high-risk classes. SATA items reward comprehensive safe nursing: select every true nursing action, not only the “most important” one. When uncertain, eliminate options that delay assessment, withhold needed escalation, or violate scope and standards of practice.

examTips: If two answers look correct, pick the action that assesses first when assessment data is missing—unless the stem already confirms a life threat that requires immediate intervention.
For medication safety, link high-alert drugs to monitoring (labs, vitals, sedation, bleeding) rather than memorizing isolated facts.
SATA: treat each option as true/false; do not stop after finding two correct answers if more are safe and indicated.
Region tip (US): read units carefully and translate glucose or lab values mentally when the stem uses mg/dL conventions.

Explain evidence-informed nursing interventions, including when to escalate care and how to communicate using SBAR.
Identify common exam distractors related to Delegation to Unlicensed Assistive Personnel (UAP/PSW) and distinguish safe nursing actions from unsafe choices.

sections.heading: Clinical overview
sections.body: Delegation to Unlicensed Assistive Personnel (UAP/PSW) is a high-yield topic for RN licensing exams because it blends pathophysiology, assessment, interventions, and safety. Nurses are tested on recognizing instability early, selecting the best first action, and anticipating complications rather than memorizing rare trivia. Keep the multi system lens in mind: trends beat single numbers, and airway–breathing–circulation threats outrank comfort tasks when priorities compete. Topic focus: Delegation tests five rights of delegation, stable versus unstable patients, tasks vs assessments, and maintaining accountability after assignment. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology and risk factors (exam lens)
sections.body: Focus on mechanisms that create predictable nursing priorities: tissue oxygen delivery, fluid shifts, inflammation, end-organ perfusion, and medication-related vulnerabilities. Exam items often pair a subtle trend (rising heart rate, narrowing pulse pressure, new confusion, or progressive work of breathing) with a distractor that sounds reasonable but delays escalation. Ask yourself what would harm the patient fastest if ignored, and what assessment data would change your next action within minutes. Reinforce: Delegation tests five rights of delegation, stable versus unstable patients, tasks vs assessments, and maintaining accountability after assignment.

sections.heading: Assessment and monitoring
sections.body: Build a mental checklist for bedside assessment: level of consciousness and airway protection, work of breathing and oxygenation, perfusion and urine output when relevant, pain and sedation interplay, infection cues, and high-alert medication effects. Tie monitoring to the plan of care: what parameter, how often, what threshold triggers notification, and what you would say in SBAR. For United States learners, be fluent in both common unit conventions used in stems and the underlying physiology so unit swaps do not break your reasoning.

sections.heading: Interventions, collaboration, and safety
sections.body: Prioritize interventions that stabilize the patient, prevent injury, and preserve dignity: oxygen and airway support as indicated, vascular access and labs as ordered, medication administration with rights verification, fall and bleeding precautions, infection prevention, and patient education that matches readiness to learn. Delegation items reward knowing what can be assigned to UAP/PSW versus what requires RN judgment, including follow-up supervision. Documentation should reflect assessments, notifications, and patient responses—not tasks performed without verification.

sections.heading: Exam strategy and distractor patterns
sections.body: Expect prioritization stems with multiple “urgent-sounding” patients: choose the option that addresses airway, breathing, circulation, or rapid neurologic decline first. For medication items, watch for reversed priorities (treating a number without symptoms), duplicate therapy, ignored allergies, and monitoring omissions for high-risk classes. SATA items reward comprehensive safe nursing: select every true nursing action, not only the “most important” one. When uncertain, eliminate options that delay assessment, withhold needed escalation, or violate scope and standards of practice.

examTips: If two answers look correct, pick the action that assesses first when assessment data is missing—unless the stem already confirms a life threat that requires immediate intervention.
For medication safety, link high-alert drugs to monitoring (labs, vitals, sedation, bleeding) rather than memorizing isolated facts.
SATA: treat each option as true/false; do not stop after finding two correct answers if more are safe and indicated.
Region tip (US): read units carefully and translate glucose or lab values mentally when the stem uses mg/dL conventions.

questions.stem: (US RN prep; myocardial-infarction; Q1) A patient is being cared for on a medical-surgical unit with a working diagnosis related to Myocardial Infarction (STEMI / NSTEMI). The nurse notes a new, objective change suggesting worsening perfusion or oxygenation. Which priority action is most appropriate first? Exam focus: High-yield cues include ischemic chest discomfort patterns, serial troponins and ECG evolution, time-to-reperfusion language, and bleeding risk when antithrombotics are used.
choices: A. Perform a focused assessment (including airway, breathing, circulation, and neuro checks) and follow unit escalation for acute changes
B. Finish non-urgent charting tasks before reassessing the patient because stable documentation is the priority
C. Ask the UAP/PSW to independently interpret the change and decide whether to call the provider
D. Tell the patient to "sleep it off" and reassess next round without additional vitals
questions.rationale: This item tests RN-level safety and clinical judgment for Myocardial Infarction (STEMI / NSTEMI) (myocardial-infarction). The correct option applies assessment-first and evidence-aligned nursing actions, including rights-based medication administration, appropriate escalation, and patient education that emphasizes red flags. Incorrect options illustrate common NCLEX distractors: delaying assessment, unsafe teaching, inappropriate delegation of judgment, or violating medication safety standards. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

incorrectRationales.B: Documentation is important, but it does not precede assessment of new objective instability; exams consistently prioritize patient assessment over administrative tasks when safety is at risk.
incorrectRationales.C: Unlicensed assistive personnel cannot replace RN assessment and clinical judgment for acute changes; the RN must evaluate, supervise, and escalate when indicated.
incorrectRationales.D: Dismissing acute changes without assessment violates standards of care and patient safety expectations on RN exams.

questions.stem: (US RN prep; myocardial-infarction; Q2) The nurse is preparing patient education before discharge for Myocardial Infarction (STEMI / NSTEMI). Which teaching statement best reflects safe, exam-style priorities? Context: High-yield cues include ischemic chest discomfort patterns, serial troponins and ECG evolution, time-to-reperfusion language, and bleeding risk when antithrombotics are used.
choices: A. Teach warning signs that require urgent evaluation and how to contact the care team, aligned with the patient's literacy and language needs
B. Advise the patient to stop all prescribed medications if they feel better to avoid side effects
C. Tell the patient to double the next dose if a dose is missed to "catch up" without contacting a provider
D. Recommend ignoring new swelling, chest pain, or sudden shortness of breath for 24 hours unless it worsens dramatically
questions.rationale: This item tests RN-level safety and clinical judgment for Myocardial Infarction (STEMI / NSTEMI) (myocardial-infarction). The correct option applies assessment-first and evidence-aligned nursing actions, including rights-based medication administration, appropriate escalation, and patient education that emphasizes red flags. Incorrect options illustrate common NCLEX distractors: delaying assessment, unsafe teaching, inappropriate delegation of judgment, or violating medication safety standards. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

incorrectRationales.B: Patients should not discontinue prescribed medications without provider guidance; this option introduces serious harm and is never the best teaching answer.
incorrectRationales.C: Doubling doses after a missed dose is unsafe for many medication classes and is not appropriate generic teaching.
incorrectRationales.D: Red-flag symptoms should prompt urgent evaluation; delaying care is clinically unsafe and fails exam expectations for patient education.

questions.stem: (US RN prep; myocardial-infarction; Q3) Select all that apply: Which nursing actions are appropriate for safe care of a patient with Myocardial Infarction (STEMI / NSTEMI)? Exam focus: High-yield cues include ischemic chest discomfort patterns, serial troponins and ECG evolution, time-to-reperfusion language, and bleeding risk when antithrombotics are used.
choices: A. Monitor trends in vitals and level of consciousness alongside ordered labs
B. Verify medications using rights of administration and high-alert precautions when applicable
C. Withhold all fluids and nutrition indefinitely without a provider order
D. Use SBAR to communicate acute changes and anticipated needs to the provider team
E. Apply infection prevention practices including hand hygiene and appropriate PPE
questions.rationale: For Myocardial Infarction (STEMI / NSTEMI), safe nursing care integrates ongoing assessment, medication safety, structured communication, and infection prevention. Monitoring trends (A) detects deterioration early. Rights verification and high-alert vigilance (B) reduce preventable harm. SBAR (D) supports timely escalation. Standard precautions and transmission-based precautions as indicated (E) reduce cross-transmission. Withholding all fluids and nutrition indefinitely (C) is not a generic nursing action without orders and clinical indication, so it is incorrect on licensing exams unless the stem explicitly supports NPO with alternative plans. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

incorrectRationales.C: Indefinite withholding of fluids and nutrition without orders and a documented plan is not a blanket appropriate action; nutrition and hydration decisions require provider direction and clinical context.

questions.stem: (US RN prep; pulmonary-embolism; Q1) A patient is being cared for on a medical-surgical unit with a working diagnosis related to Deep Vein Thrombosis and Pulmonary Embolism. The nurse notes a new, objective change suggesting worsening perfusion or oxygenation. Which priority action is most appropriate first? Exam focus: Expect questions on sudden dyspnea, tachycardia, risk factors, oxygenation, anticoagulation safety, and bleeding precautions—not memorizing a single diagnostic algorithm in isolation.
choices: A. Perform a focused assessment (including airway, breathing, circulation, and neuro checks) and follow unit escalation for acute changes
B. Finish non-urgent charting tasks before reassessing the patient because stable documentation is the priority
C. Ask the UAP/PSW to independently interpret the change and decide whether to call the provider
D. Tell the patient to "sleep it off" and reassess next round without additional vitals
questions.rationale: This item tests RN-level safety and clinical judgment for Deep Vein Thrombosis and Pulmonary Embolism (pulmonary-embolism). The correct option applies assessment-first and evidence-aligned nursing actions, including rights-based medication administration, appropriate escalation, and patient education that emphasizes red flags. Incorrect options illustrate common NCLEX distractors: delaying assessment, unsafe teaching, inappropriate delegation of judgment, or violating medication safety standards. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

incorrectRationales.B: Documentation is important, but it does not precede assessment of new objective instability; exams consistently prioritize patient assessment over administrative tasks when safety is at risk.
incorrectRationales.C: Unlicensed assistive personnel cannot replace RN assessment and clinical judgment for acute changes; the RN must evaluate, supervise, and escalate when indicated.
incorrectRationales.D: Dismissing acute changes without assessment violates standards of care and patient safety expectations on RN exams.

questions.stem: (US RN prep; pulmonary-embolism; Q2) The nurse is preparing patient education before discharge for Deep Vein Thrombosis and Pulmonary Embolism. Which teaching statement best reflects safe, exam-style priorities? Context: Expect questions on sudden dyspnea, tachycardia, risk factors, oxygenation, anticoagulation safety, and bleeding precautions—not memorizing a single diagnostic algorithm in isolation.
choices: A. Teach warning signs that require urgent evaluation and how to contact the care team, aligned with the patient's literacy and language needs
B. Advise the patient to stop all prescribed medications if they feel better to avoid side effects
C. Tell the patient to double the next dose if a dose is missed to "catch up" without contacting a provider
D. Recommend ignoring new swelling, chest pain, or sudden shortness of breath for 24 hours unless it worsens dramatically

questions.stem: (CA RN prep; acute-kidney-injury; Q3) Select all that apply: Which nursing actions are appropriate for safe care of a patient with Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)? questions.stem: (US RN prep; liver-cirrhosis-hepatic-encephalopathy; Q1) A patient is being cared for on a medical-surgical unit with a working diagnosis related to Liver Cirrhosis and Hepatic Encephalopathy. The nurse notes a new, objective change suggesting worsening perfusion or oxygenation. Which priority action is most appropriate first? Exam focus: Cirrhosis bundles include ascites infection suspicion, variceal bleed risk, ammonia as an imperfect marker, lactulose/rifaximin concepts, and bleeding coagulopathy framing.
choices: A. Perform a focused assessment (including airway, breathing, circulation, and neuro checks) and follow unit escalation for acute changes
B. Finish non-urgent charting tasks before reassessing the patient because stable documentation is the priority
C. Ask the UAP/PSW to independently interpret the change and decide whether to call the provider
D. Tell the patient to "sleep it off" and reassess next round without additional vitals

questions.stem: (CA RN prep; heart-failure; Q3) Which nursing action best demonstrates safe medication and monitoring practice for Heart Failure (Systolic and Diastolic)? Hint: HF exams mix HFrEF/HFpEF recognition, daily weights, orthopnea/PND, diuretic response, and exacerbation triggers including diet and medication adherence.
choices: A. Verify the medication order, confirm patient identity, assess relevant vitals/labs per protocol, and document administration and patient response
B. Administer a PRN sedative first whenever the patient seems anxious, without reassessing respiratory status
C. Crush enteric-coated or extended-release medications whenever administration is easier for the nurse
D. Skip allergy verification if the patient has received the medication before on a prior admission
questions.rationale: This item tests RN-level safety and clinical judgment for Heart Failure (Systolic and Diastolic) (heart-failure). incorrectRationales.B: Sedation decisions require assessment of respiratory status and indication; indiscriminate PRN use is unsafe.
incorrectRationales.C: Altering formulation without an order and pharmacist guidance can change absorption and cause harm.
incorrectRationales.D: Allergy verification is a standard safety step; prior tolerance does not remove the need to verify each administration cycle per policy.

questions.stem: (US RN prep; atrial-fibrillation; Q4) Select all that apply: Which nursing actions are appropriate for safe care of a patient with Atrial Fibrillation and Rhythm Management? Exam focus: AFib items emphasize irregularly irregular rhythm recognition, rate versus rhythm control concepts, stroke risk framing, and anticoagulation safety/monitoring—not casual cardioversion without context.
choices: A. Monitor trends in vitals and level of consciousness alongside ordered labs

E. Apply infection prevention practices including hand hygiene and appropriate PPE

questions.stem: (CA RN prep; pneumonia; Q1) A patient is being cared for on a medical-surgical unit with a working diagnosis related to Community-Acquired and Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia. The nurse notes a new, objective change suggesting worsening perfusion or oxygenation. Which priority action is most appropriate first? Exam focus: Pneumonia care is tested through oxygenation, cultures before antibiotics when feasible, aspiration risk, CURB-65 style severity language, and vaccine education opportunities.
choices: A. Perform a focused assessment (including airway, breathing, circulation, and neuro checks) and follow unit escalation for acute changes
B. Finish non-urgent charting tasks before reassessing the patient because stable documentation is the priority
C. Ask the UAP/PSW to independently interpret the change and decide whether to call the provider
D. Tell the patient to "sleep it off" and reassess next round without additional vitals
questions.rationale: This item tests RN-level safety and clinical judgment for Community-Acquired and Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia (pneumonia). questions.stem: (US RN prep; acute-pancreatitis; Q3) Which nursing action best demonstrates safe medication and monitoring practice for Acute Pancreatitis? questions.stem: (US RN prep; acute-pancreatitis; Q4) Select all that apply: Which nursing actions are appropriate for safe care of a patient with Acute Pancreatitis? Exam focus: Pancreatitis items emphasize pain control, NPO with nutrition escalation concepts, alcohol and gallstone triggers, and monitoring for systemic inflammatory complications.
choices: A. Monitor trends in vitals and level of consciousness alongside ordered labs
B. Verify medications using rights of administration and high-alert precautions when applicable
C. Withhold all fluids and nutrition indefinitely without a provider order
D. Use SBAR to communicate acute changes and anticipated needs to the provider team
E. Apply infection prevention practices including hand hygiene and appropriate PPE
questions.rationale: For Acute Pancreatitis, safe nursing care integrates ongoing assessment, medication safety, structured communication, and infection prevention. Monitoring trends (A) detects deterioration early. Rights verification and high-alert vigilance (B) reduce preventable harm. SBAR (D) supports timely escalation. Standard precautions and transmission-based precautions as indicated (E) reduce cross-transmission. Withholding all fluids and nutrition indefinitely (C) is not a generic nursing action without orders and clinical indication, so it is incorrect on licensing exams unless the stem explicitly supports NPO with alternative plans. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

incorrectRationales.C: Indefinite withholding of fluids and nutrition without orders and a documented plan is not a blanket appropriate action; nutrition and hydration decisions require provider direction and clinical context.

questions.stem: (CA RN prep; inflammatory-bowel-disease; Q1) A patient is being cared for on a medical-surgical unit with a working diagnosis related to Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn's and Ulcerative Colitis. questions.stem: (US RN prep; appendicitis-and-peritonitis; Q4) Select all that apply: Which nursing actions are appropriate for safe care of a patient with Appendicitis and Peritonitis? Exam focus: Appendicitis/peritonitis tests rebound guarding language, NPO readiness, pain reassessment, sudden pain relief as a possible perforation clue, and surgical collaboration.
choices: A. Monitor trends in vitals and level of consciousness alongside ordered labs
B. Verify medications using rights of administration and high-alert precautions when applicable
C. Withhold all fluids and nutrition indefinitely without a provider order
D. Use SBAR to communicate acute changes and anticipated needs to the provider team
E. Apply infection prevention practices including hand hygiene and appropriate PPE
questions.rationale: For Appendicitis and Peritonitis, safe nursing care integrates ongoing assessment, medication safety, structured communication, and infection prevention. Monitoring trends (A) detects deterioration early. Rights verification and high-alert vigilance (B) reduce preventable harm. SBAR (D) supports timely escalation. Standard precautions and transmission-based precautions as indicated (E) reduce cross-transmission. Withholding all fluids and nutrition indefinitely (C) is not a generic nursing action without orders and clinical indication, so it is incorrect on licensing exams unless the stem explicitly supports NPO with alternative plans. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

incorrectRationales.C: Indefinite withholding of fluids and nutrition without orders and a documented plan is not a blanket appropriate action; nutrition and hydration decisions require provider direction and clinical context.

questions.stem: (US RN prep; anticoagulation-therapy; Q1) A patient is being cared for on a medical-surgical unit with a working diagnosis related to Anticoagulation: Heparin, Warfarin, and DOACs. The nurse notes a new, objective change suggesting worsening perfusion or oxygenation. Which priority action is most appropriate first? Exam focus: Anticoagulation items test aPTT/INR concepts as commonly used, bleeding precautions, reversal as a principle, DOAC renal considerations, and peri-procedure communication.
choices: A. Perform a focused assessment (including airway, breathing, circulation, and neuro checks) and follow unit escalation for acute changes
B. Finish non-urgent charting tasks before reassessing the patient because stable documentation is the priority
C. Ask the UAP/PSW to independently interpret the change and decide whether to call the provider
D. Tell the patient to "sleep it off" and reassess next round without additional vitals
questions.rationale: This item tests RN-level safety and clinical judgment for Anticoagulation: Heparin, Warfarin, and DOACs (anticoagulation-therapy). The correct option applies assessment-first and evidence-aligned nursing actions, including rights-based medication administration, appropriate escalation, and patient education that emphasizes red flags. Incorrect options illustrate common NCLEX distractors: delaying assessment, unsafe teaching, inappropriate delegation of judgment, or violating medication safety standards. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

incorrectRationales.B: Documentation is important, but it does not precede assessment of new objective instability; exams consistently prioritize patient assessment over administrative tasks when safety is at risk.

incorrectRationales.D: Dismissing acute changes without assessment violates standards of care and patient safety expectations on RN exams.

questions.stem: (US RN prep; anticoagulation-therapy; Q2) The nurse is preparing patient education before discharge for Anticoagulation: Heparin, Warfarin, and DOACs. choices: A. Teach warning signs that require urgent evaluation and how to contact the care team, aligned with the patient's literacy and language needs

questions.stem: (US RN prep; anticoagulation-therapy; Q3) Select all that apply: Which nursing actions are appropriate for safe care of a patient with Anticoagulation: Heparin, Warfarin, and DOACs? choices: A. Monitor trends in vitals and level of consciousness alongside ordered labs

questions.stem: (CA RN prep; insulin-management; Q3) Which nursing action best demonstrates safe medication and monitoring practice for Insulin Types, Administration, and Hypoglycemia Management? Hint: Insulin safety focuses on hypoglycemia recognition, correction insulin cautions, basal-bolus concepts, and sick-day rules as patient education points.
choices: A. Verify the medication order, confirm patient identity, assess relevant vitals/labs per protocol, and document administration and patient response
B. Administer a PRN sedative first whenever the patient seems anxious, without reassessing respiratory status
C. Crush enteric-coated or extended-release medications whenever administration is easier for the nurse
D. Skip allergy verification if the patient has received the medication before on a prior admission
questions.rationale: This item tests RN-level safety and clinical judgment for Insulin Types, Administration, and Hypoglycemia Management (insulin-management). This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

incorrectRationales.B: Sedation decisions require assessment of respiratory status and indication; indiscriminate PRN use is unsafe.
incorrectRationales.C: Altering formulation without an order and pharmacist guidance can change absorption and cause harm.
incorrectRationales.D: Allergy verification is a standard safety step; prior tolerance does not remove the need to verify each administration cycle per policy.

questions.stem: (CA RN prep; antibiotic-selection-and-safety; Q4) Select all that apply: Which nursing actions are appropriate for safe care of a patient with Antibiotic Selection, Safety, and Allergy Management? Exam focus: Antibiotic stems include allergy verification, culture timing, C. difficile risk communication, renal dosing as a principle, and finishing courses as education—not blanket advice.
choices: A. Monitor trends in vitals and level of consciousness alongside ordered labs
B. Verify medications using rights of administration and high-alert precautions when applicable
C. Withhold all fluids and nutrition indefinitely without a provider order
D. Use SBAR to communicate acute changes and anticipated needs to the provider team
E. Apply infection prevention practices including hand hygiene and appropriate PPE
questions.rationale: For Antibiotic Selection, Safety, and Allergy Management, safe nursing care integrates ongoing assessment, medication safety, structured communication, and infection prevention. Monitoring trends (A) detects deterioration early. Rights verification and high-alert vigilance (B) reduce preventable harm. SBAR (D) supports timely escalation. Standard precautions and transmission-based precautions as indicated (E) reduce cross-transmission. Withholding all fluids and nutrition indefinitely (C) is not a generic nursing action without orders and clinical indication, so it is incorrect on licensing exams unless the stem explicitly supports NPO with alternative plans. Use Canadian context where relevant: SI labs (mmol/L for glucose when applicable), kPa for blood gases when used, CTAS triage language for emergency scenarios, RPN/LPN scope paired with RN accountability, and provincial interprofessional standards. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

incorrectRationales.C: Indefinite withholding of fluids and nutrition without orders and a documented plan is not a blanket appropriate action; nutrition and hydration decisions require provider direction and clinical context.

questions.stem: (CA RN prep; antipsychotic-medications; Q1) A patient is being cared for on a medical-surgical unit with a working diagnosis related to Antipsychotic Medications: Typical, Atypical, and EPS. choices: A. Perform a focused assessment (including airway, breathing, circulation, and neuro checks) and follow unit escalation for acute changes

incorrectRationales.B: Documentation is important, but it does not precede assessment of new objective instability; exams consistently prioritize patient assessment over administrative tasks when safety is at risk.

incorrectRationales.D: Dismissing acute changes without assessment violates standards of care and patient safety expectations on RN exams.

questions.stem: (CA RN prep; antipsychotic-medications; Q2) The nurse is preparing patient education before discharge for Antipsychotic Medications: Typical, Atypical, and EPS. questions.stem: (CA RN prep; antipsychotic-medications; Q3) Which nursing action best demonstrates safe medication and monitoring practice for Antipsychotic Medications: Typical, Atypical, and EPS? choices: A. Verify the medication order, confirm patient identity, assess relevant vitals/labs per protocol, and document administration and patient response

questions.rationale: This item tests RN-level safety and clinical judgment for Antipsychotic Medications: Typical, Atypical, and EPS (antipsychotic-medications). incorrectRationales.B: Sedation decisions require assessment of respiratory status and indication; indiscriminate PRN use is unsafe.

questions.stem: (CA RN prep; multiple-patient-assignment; Q3) Select all that apply: Which nursing actions are appropriate for safe care of a patient with Multiple Patient Assignment: Prioritization and Workload? questions.stem: (US RN prep; airway-management-priority; Q1) A patient is being cared for on a medical-surgical unit with a working diagnosis related to Airway Management and Priority Interventions. The nurse notes a new, objective change suggesting worsening perfusion or oxygenation. Which priority action is most appropriate first? Exam focus: Airway questions test jaw thrust/chin lift as concepts, suctioning indications, positioning, calling for help early, and avoiding food/fluid if aspiration risk is high.
choices: A. Perform a focused assessment (including airway, breathing, circulation, and neuro checks) and follow unit escalation for acute changes
B. Finish non-urgent charting tasks before reassessing the patient because stable documentation is the priority
C. Ask the UAP/PSW to independently interpret the change and decide whether to call the provider
D. Tell the patient to "sleep it off" and reassess next round without additional vitals
questions.rationale: This item tests RN-level safety and clinical judgment for Airway Management and Priority Interventions (airway-management-priority). The correct option applies assessment-first and evidence-aligned nursing actions, including rights-based medication administration, appropriate escalation, and patient education that emphasizes red flags. Incorrect options illustrate common NCLEX distractors: delaying assessment, unsafe teaching, inappropriate delegation of judgment, or violating medication safety standards. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

incorrectRationales.B: Documentation is important, but it does not precede assessment of new objective instability; exams consistently prioritize patient assessment over administrative tasks when safety is at risk.
incorrectRationales.C: Unlicensed assistive personnel cannot replace RN assessment and clinical judgment for acute changes; the RN must evaluate, supervise, and escalate when indicated.
incorrectRationales.D: Dismissing acute changes without assessment violates standards of care and patient safety expectations on RN exams.

incorrectRationales.B: Sedation decisions require assessment of respiratory status and indication; indiscriminate PRN use is unsafe.
incorrectRationales.C: Altering formulation without an order and pharmacist guidance can change absorption and cause harm.
incorrectRationales.D: Allergy verification is a standard safety step; prior tolerance does not remove the need to verify each administration cycle per policy.

questions.stem: (CA RN prep; fall-prevention-and-safety; Q3) Which nursing action best demonstrates safe medication and monitoring practice for Fall Prevention: Risk Assessment and Safety Interventions? questions.stem: (US RN prep; surgical-site-infection-prevention; Q4) Select all that apply: Which nursing actions are appropriate for safe care of a patient with Surgical Site Infection Prevention and Wound Assessment? Exam focus: SSI prevention tests sterile field respect, glycemic control as a concept, hair removal principles, and wound assessment for infection signs.
choices: A. Monitor trends in vitals and level of consciousness alongside ordered labs
B. Verify medications using rights of administration and high-alert precautions when applicable
C. Withhold all fluids and nutrition indefinitely without a provider order
D. Use SBAR to communicate acute changes and anticipated needs to the provider team
E. Apply infection prevention practices including hand hygiene and appropriate PPE
questions.rationale: For Surgical Site Infection Prevention and Wound Assessment, safe nursing care integrates ongoing assessment, medication safety, structured communication, and infection prevention. Monitoring trends (A) detects deterioration early. Rights verification and high-alert vigilance (B) reduce preventable harm. SBAR (D) supports timely escalation. Standard precautions and transmission-based precautions as indicated (E) reduce cross-transmission. Withholding all fluids and nutrition indefinitely (C) is not a generic nursing action without orders and clinical indication, so it is incorrect on licensing exams unless the stem explicitly supports NPO with alternative plans. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

incorrectRationales.C: Indefinite withholding of fluids and nutrition without orders and a documented plan is not a blanket appropriate action; nutrition and hydration decisions require provider direction and clinical context.

incorrectRationales.B: Documentation is important, but it does not precede assessment of new objective instability; exams consistently prioritize patient assessment over administrative tasks when safety is at risk.
incorrectRationales.C: Unlicensed assistive personnel cannot replace RN assessment and clinical judgment for acute changes; the RN must evaluate, supervise, and escalate when indicated.
incorrectRationales.D: Dismissing acute changes without assessment violates standards of care and patient safety expectations on RN exams.

questions.stem: (US RN prep; delegation-to-uap; Q2) The nurse is preparing patient education before discharge for Delegation to Unlicensed Assistive Personnel (UAP/PSW). Which teaching statement best reflects safe, exam-style priorities? Context: Delegation tests five rights of delegation, stable versus unstable patients, tasks vs assessments, and maintaining accountability after assignment.
choices: A. Teach warning signs that require urgent evaluation and how to contact the care team, aligned with the patient's literacy and language needs
B. Advise the patient to stop all prescribed medications if they feel better to avoid side effects
C. Tell the patient to double the next dose if a dose is missed to "catch up" without contacting a provider
D. Recommend ignoring new swelling, chest pain, or sudden shortness of breath for 24 hours unless it worsens dramatically
questions.rationale: This item tests RN-level safety and clinical judgment for Delegation to Unlicensed Assistive Personnel (UAP/PSW) (delegation-to-uap). The correct option applies assessment-first and evidence-aligned nursing actions, including rights-based medication administration, appropriate escalation, and patient education that emphasizes red flags. Incorrect options illustrate common NCLEX distractors: delaying assessment, unsafe teaching, inappropriate delegation of judgment, or violating medication safety standards. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

incorrectRationales.B: Patients should not discontinue prescribed medications without provider guidance; this option introduces serious harm and is never the best teaching answer.
incorrectRationales.C: Doubling doses after a missed dose is unsafe for many medication classes and is not appropriate generic teaching.
incorrectRationales.D: Red-flag symptoms should prompt urgent evaluation; delaying care is clinically unsafe and fails exam expectations for patient education.

questions.stem: (US RN prep; delegation-to-uap; Q3) Which nursing action best demonstrates safe medication and monitoring practice for Delegation to Unlicensed Assistive Personnel (UAP/PSW)? Hint: Delegation tests five rights of delegation, stable versus unstable patients, tasks vs assessments, and maintaining accountability after assignment.
choices: A. Verify the medication order, confirm patient identity, assess relevant vitals/labs per protocol, and document administration and patient response
B. Administer a PRN sedative first whenever the patient seems anxious, without reassessing respiratory status
C. Crush enteric-coated or extended-release medications whenever administration is easier for the nurse
D. Skip allergy verification if the patient has received the medication before on a prior admission
questions.rationale: This item tests RN-level safety and clinical judgment for Delegation to Unlicensed Assistive Personnel (UAP/PSW) (delegation-to-uap). The correct option applies assessment-first and evidence-aligned nursing actions, including rights-based medication administration, appropriate escalation, and patient education that emphasizes red flags. Incorrect options illustrate common NCLEX distractors: delaying assessment, unsafe teaching, inappropriate delegation of judgment, or violating medication safety standards. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

incorrectRationales.B: Sedation decisions require assessment of respiratory status and indication; indiscriminate PRN use is unsafe.
incorrectRationales.C: Altering formulation without an order and pharmacist guidance can change absorption and cause harm.
incorrectRationales.D: Allergy verification is a standard safety step; prior tolerance does not remove the need to verify each administration cycle per policy.

questions.stem: (US RN prep; delegation-to-uap; Q4) Select all that apply: Which nursing actions are appropriate for safe care of a patient with Delegation to Unlicensed Assistive Personnel (UAP/PSW)? Exam focus: Delegation tests five rights of delegation, stable versus unstable patients, tasks vs assessments, and maintaining accountability after assignment.
choices: A. Monitor trends in vitals and level of consciousness alongside ordered labs
B. Verify medications using rights of administration and high-alert precautions when applicable
C. Withhold all fluids and nutrition indefinitely without a provider order
D. Use SBAR to communicate acute changes and anticipated needs to the provider team
E. Apply infection prevention practices including hand hygiene and appropriate PPE
questions.rationale: For Delegation to Unlicensed Assistive Personnel (UAP/PSW), safe nursing care integrates ongoing assessment, medication safety, structured communication, and infection prevention. Monitoring trends (A) detects deterioration early. Rights verification and high-alert vigilance (B) reduce preventable harm. SBAR (D) supports timely escalation. Standard precautions and transmission-based precautions as indicated (E) reduce cross-transmission. Withholding all fluids and nutrition indefinitely (C) is not a generic nursing action without orders and clinical indication, so it is incorrect on licensing exams unless the stem explicitly supports NPO with alternative plans. Use US NCLEX-RN context where relevant: common US units (mg/dL glucose when applicable), START triage language for mass-casualty style stems, UAP scope language, and Joint Commission–style safety framing. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

incorrectRationales.C: Indefinite withholding of fluids and nutrition without orders and a documented plan is not a blanket appropriate action; nutrition and hydration decisions require provider direction and clinical context.

questions.stem: (CA RN prep; lpn-rpn-scope-of-practice; Q1) A patient is being cared for on a medical-surgical unit with a working diagnosis related to LPN/RPN Scope of Practice and RN Collaboration. The nurse notes a new, objective change suggesting worsening perfusion or oxygenation. Which priority action is most appropriate first? Exam focus: RN/RPN(LPN) collaboration items emphasize scope boundaries, teaching versus assigning judgment tasks, and shared accountability in team models.
choices: A. Perform a focused assessment (including airway, breathing, circulation, and neuro checks) and follow unit escalation for acute changes
B. Finish non-urgent charting tasks before reassessing the patient because stable documentation is the priority
C. Ask the UAP/PSW to independently interpret the change and decide whether to call the provider
D. Tell the patient to "sleep it off" and reassess next round without additional vitals
questions.rationale: This item tests RN-level safety and clinical judgment for LPN/RPN Scope of Practice and RN Collaboration (lpn-rpn-scope-of-practice). The correct option applies assessment-first and evidence-aligned nursing actions, including rights-based medication administration, appropriate escalation, and patient education that emphasizes red flags. Incorrect options illustrate common NCLEX distractors: delaying assessment, unsafe teaching, inappropriate delegation of judgment, or violating medication safety standards. Use Canadian context where relevant: SI labs (mmol/L for glucose when applicable), kPa for blood gases when used, CTAS triage language for emergency scenarios, RPN/LPN scope paired with RN accountability, and provincial interprofessional standards. This is exam preparation content, not bedside medical orders.

incorrectRationales.C: Unlicensed assistive personnel cannot replace RN assessment and clinical judgment for acute changes; the RN must evaluate, supervise, and escalate when indicated.
incorrectRationales.D: Dismissing acute changes without assessment violates standards of care and patient safety expectations on RN exams.
